Winterthur

ZHZurich

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The lost road to Vitudurum

A child discovers Roman coins near St. Arbogast church and follows the ancient road back in time to the bustling vicus of Vitudurum. They meet a Roman potter, visit the thermal baths, and witness the construction of the castrum wall in 294 AD as the old world gives way to the new.

Create This Story

The name Winterthur traces back to the Celtic settlement Uitoduro, meaning roughly pasture gate. The Romans Latinised it to Vitudurum around 280 AD, establishing a vicus at present-day Oberwinterthur. Roman timber buildings have been dendrochronologically dated to around 4 BC. The settlement flourished with a temple, thermal baths, and a water supply system. During the Alemannic invasions, the vicus was replaced by a fortified castrum on the St. Arbogast hill, dated to 294 AD.

The bear on the Kyburg tower

A young squire in Duke Ulrich’s castle must warn the people of Winterthur about an approaching danger. Racing down the Töss valley from the Kyburg tower, the child discovers what it means to protect a newly founded city — and why some walls are worth defending.

Create This Story

Kyburg Castle overlooks the Töss valley, about 3 km south-east of Winterthur. First fortified in the 10th century, the modern castle dates to ca. 1200 when Duke Ulrich III von Kyburg ordered its construction. The Kyburgs founded Winterthur as a city around 1175. When the Kyburg male line went extinct in 1264, their holdings passed to the House of Habsburg. The castle is the best-preserved feudal keep in north-eastern Switzerland.

The day the music started

Set in 1629, a child joins clergyman Hans Heinrich Meyer’s new Musikkollegium, learning to sing and play. Each rehearsal unlocks a different room in the Stadtkirche, where centuries of music echo from the twin towers — from Baroque cantatas to Stravinsky’s modern harmonies.

Create This Story

The Musikkollegium Winterthur was founded in 1629 by clergyman Hans Heinrich Meyer, making it one of the oldest continuously operating musical institutions in Europe. Originally a choir of 30–40 members who each had to sing and play an instrument. In the early 20th century, patron Werner Reinhart and conductor Hermann Scherchen turned Winterthur into a European musical hub, hosting Stravinsky, Richard Strauss, and Anton Webern.

The turbocharger kid

Young Alfred Büchi is fascinated by the roaring engines at the Sulzer factory. A magical gear transports him inside a diesel engine, where he must figure out how to harness the wasted exhaust gas — and invents the turbocharger that will power ships and machines across the world.

Create This Story

Alfred Büchi (1879–1959), born in Winterthur, invented the exhaust-gas turbocharger, patenting it in 1905. His work at Sulzer and the Büchi Syndicate revolutionised diesel engines worldwide. Sulzer, founded in 1834 by Johann Jakob Sulzer-Neuffert, became one of the great engine manufacturers. Rudolf Diesel himself worked at Sulzer in 1879, and Sulzer built its first diesel engine in 1898. The city named Alfred-Büchi-Weg in his honour in 2012.

The painter's villa

A child visiting the Am Römerholz collection steps into a painting by Bruegel and must find their way home by travelling through canvases — from Cranach’s forests to Renoir’s sun-dappled gardens to Van Gogh’s starry night — discovering how art connects centuries.

Create This Story

The Sammlung Oskar Reinhart Am Römerholz is a world-class art museum in the collector’s 1915 villa. It contains 207 works spanning Late Gothic to early 20th century, including Cranach the Elder, Bruegel the Elder, El Greco, Renoir, Cézanne, Manet, van Gogh, and early Picasso. Oskar Reinhart (1885–1965), son of trading magnate Theodor Reinhart, bequeathed the collection to the Swiss Confederation; it has been open to the public since 1970.

500 experiments

During a school trip to Technorama, a child accidentally activates all 500 exhibits at once. To set things right, they must solve scientific puzzles across three floors before the museum opens the next morning — a race against time through physics, chemistry, and wonder.

Create This Story

The Swiss Science Center Technorama is the only science centre in Switzerland and one of the largest in Europe, with over 500 hands-on exhibits across 6,500 m² on three floors. Over 64,000 schoolchildren visit annually. Located in Oberwinterthur, it was expanded in 2021 with Technorama Outdoors. The centre encourages learning through direct experimentation and play.

The goalkeeper from Winterthur

Young Hans Gamper kicks a ball through the Marktgasse cobblestones and dreams of a football club. The story follows him from Winterthur’s medieval lanes to co-founding FC Zurich and then founding FC Barcelona in 1899 — showing how a Swiss boy changed world football forever.

Create This Story

Joan Gamper (Hans-Max Gamper, 1877–1930), born in Winterthur, co-founded FC Zurich in 1896 and then founded FC Barcelona in 1899. He served as Barcelona’s president five times and scored over 100 goals in 48 matches. The annual Trofeo Joan Gamper pre-season tournament honours his legacy. He remains one of the most significant figures in football history to come from Switzerland.

The secret room in Schloss Hegi

A child exploring the 800-year-old Schloss Hegi discovers the bishop’s hidden living room from 1496. Inside, a medieval riddle sends them on a quest through all three of Winterthur’s castles — Hegi, Kyburg, and Mörsburg — each holding a piece of the puzzle.

Create This Story

Schloss Hegi was first mentioned in 1225. Its core is a defensive residential tower with a moat, built around 1200 to secure property of the Bishopric of Constance. The Lords of Hohenlandenberg expanded it from 1460 with ring walls and a half-timber residence with chapel. The bishop’s living room (1496) is one of the most important medieval rooms in eastern Switzerland. Winterthur also has Kyburg Castle and the Mörsburg, giving the city three remarkable medieval castles.
